> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Data Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Failure Data Analysis (FDA)** is the systematic statistical and engineering process of collecting, classifying, modelling, and interpreting data pertaining to the time-to-failure (or number-of-cycles-to-failure) of components, sub-assemblies, or systems, in order to quantify their reliability characteristics, identify failure mechanisms, predict future performance, and support decisions on warranty, maintenance, and design improvement.

In reliability engineering, failure data forms the **empirical backbone** of every reliability prediction. Without well-organised data, even the most elegant mathematical reliability model (Weibull, Exponential, Lognormal) is reduced to a *theoretical abstraction* with no engineering value.

### 1.2 Categories of Failure Data

KTU syllabus requires the engineer to distinguish between the *quality* of the input data because each type mandates a different analytical tool.

| Data Class | Description | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Complete Data** | Every unit has failed by the end of the test. | Life-testing 50 bulbs until all burn out. |
| **Right-Censored (Suspended) Data** | Some units are still running at test termination. | 20 motors, 12 failed, 8 still alive at 5,000 h. |
| **Interval-Censored (Grouped) Data** | Failure known only to lie within a window. | Inspection of bearings every 250 h. |
| **Left-Censored Data** | Failure occurred before observation began. | Field warranty returns with unknown service time. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Hospital Triage" Model)

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a **hospital emergency ward** that records the exact time every admitted patient either *recovers* (success) or *dies* (failure). Some patients are still under treatment when the doctor's shift ends — these are the **suspended cases**. A statistician cannot simply *ignore* the still-admitted patients (that would bias the death-rate upward), nor can they assume all survivors would have died. Failure Data Analysis is precisely the statistical "triage" that uses mathematical methods (Kaplan–Meier, Actuarial, Weibull plotting) to **fairly extract the maximum reliability information** from a mix of completed and incomplete patient records.

### 1.4 Why Failure Data Analysis is a *KTU High-Weightage Topic*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 4 of PERUT636 transforms abstract reliability functions $R(t)$, $F(t)$, $h(t)$ and $\lambda(t)$ into **numerically quantifiable engineering parameters**. The board examiner's perspective: *a student who can analyse a small dataset of 10–20 failure times and produce a usable Weibull shape parameter $\beta$ and scale parameter $\eta$ is ready to work in any Industry 4.0 reliability group.*

### 1.5 Engineering Significance (Physical Constants & Metrics)

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** $\theta = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ (h or cycles).
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** for non-repairable items, identical to MTBF.
- **Hazard Rate $\lambda(t)$:** instantaneous failure rate, **units = 1/time**.
- **Reliability Function $R(t)$:** **dimensionless** probability $\in [0,1]$.
- **B10 Life:** time at which **10% of the population has failed** — heavily used in bearing and aerospace specs.
- **L10 Life:** classical bearing rating life (now superseded by L10 in ISO 281:2007).
- **Characteristic Life $\eta$ (Weibull):** time at which 63.2% of items have failed.
- **Acceleration Factor (AF):** dimensionless multiplier relating stress levels, governed by the **Arrhenius equation** for temperature:

$$ AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_{use}}-\frac{1}{T_{acc}}\right)\right] $$

where $E_a$ is the activation energy (eV), $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant = $8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K**, and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Logical Steps of Failure Data Analysis

A KTU board answer that demonstrates clear methodological structure scores far higher than a vague recollection of formulas. Use the following seven-step pipeline as the analytical backbone.

1. **Data Acquisition & Cleaning** — record the exact failure times (or suspension times), batch/serial numbers, operating conditions, and censoring flags.
2. **Data Classification** — sort into complete, right-censored, and interval-censored subsets.
3. **Choice of Non-Parametric vs Parametric Approach**:
   - Use **non-parametric** (Kaplan–Meier, Actuarial) when no distribution can be assumed *a priori* or sample size $n$ is small ($n<20$).
   - Use **parametric** (Weibull, Exponential, Lognormal) when $n \ge 20$ and physical failure physics supports a model.
4. **Ranking of Failures** — assign failure order numbers $i = 1, 2, \dots, r$ and compute the plotting position $F_i$.
5. **Probability Plotting** — plot the failure data on the appropriate probability paper; perform linear regression to extract distribution parameters.
6. **Parameter Estimation Refinement** — use **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** to refine the graphical estimates; MLE is *asymptotically efficient, unbiased, and consistent*.
7. **Goodness-of-Fit & Confidence Bounds** — apply the **Anderson–Darling** or **Kolmogorov–Smirnov** statistic; report two-sided 90% confidence bounds on $R(t)$.

### 2.2 The Why-How of Plotting Positions

> [!TIP]
> **Plotting position** = the empirical estimate of the cumulative failure probability $F(t_i)$ assigned to the $i$-th ordered failure. Different formulas are used because each represents a different assumption about the *prior* probability that a unit will fail in the next instant.

The three most-used KTU-grade formulas are:

- **Mean Rank (Brom's standard for non-censored data):**

$$ F_i^{MR} = \frac{i}{n+1} $$

- **Median Rank (preferred — Hazen / Benard approximation, more accurate for small $n$):**

$$ F_i^{MdR} = \frac{i - 0.3}{n + 0.4} $$

- **Mean Rank with Suspensions (used when censoring exists, in conjunction with Kaplan–Meier):**

$$ F_i^{MR,s} = \frac{i}{N_{i}+1} $$

where $N_i$ is the *risk set* (number still operating just before the $i$-th failure).

### 2.3 The "Why" of Censoring Correction

A censored observation is *informative* — it tells us the unit survived until time $t_{s}$. If we naïvely ignored it, our **hazard rate would be biased upward**. Methods like the **Actuarial method** (intervals) and **Kaplan–Meier product-limit** (point failures) preserve this information by using the *conditional survival probability* in each interval.

### 2.4 Parametric Distribution Candidates — Selection Logic

| Failure Physics | Recommended Distribution | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Random, memoryless failures | **Exponential** | Constant hazard $\lambda(t)=\lambda$ |
| Wear-out, fatigue, crack growth | **Weibull** ($\beta > 1$) | Increasing hazard captures degradation |
| Early-life (infant mortality) | **Weibull** ($\beta < 1$) | Decreasing hazard |
| Repair-time, multiplicative stress | **Lognormal** | Multiplication of small random effects → log-normal |
| Many independent weak-link failures | **Extreme-Value / Weibull** | Minima of many random variables |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the entries in the table below. They collectively cover $\ge 90\%$ of the numerical questions asked in PERUT636 module-end and ESE papers.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability function | $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Exponential CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda > 0$, $1/\text{time}$ |
| 3 | Exponential reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Exponential PDF | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $1/\text{time}$ |
| 5 | Exponential MTTF | $MTTF = 1/\lambda$ | time |
| 6 | Weibull CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $t \ge 0$, $\beta, \eta > 0$ |
| 7 | Weibull PDF | $f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $1/\text{time}$ |
| 8 | Weibull reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Weibull hazard | $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $1/\text{time}$ |
| 10 | Weibull characteristic life | $t_{0.632} = \eta$ | time |
| 11 | Weibull MTTF | $MTTF = \eta \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | time; $\Gamma$ = gamma fn. |
| 12 | Lognormal CDF | $F(t) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{\ln t - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\Phi$ = standard normal CDF |
| 13 | Lognormal MTTF | $MTTF = e^{\mu + \sigma^{2}/2}$ | time |
| 14 | Median Rank | $F_i = (i - 0.3)/(n + 0.4)$ | empirical CDF |
| 15 | Mean Rank | $F_i = i/(n+1)$ | empirical CDF |
| 16 | Arrhenius AF | $AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_u}-\frac{1}{T_a}\right)\right]$ | dimensionless |
| 17 | Kaplan–Meier | $\hat{R}(t_k) = \prod_{t_i \le t_k}\!\left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right)$ | step function |
| 18 | Two-sided 90% CI on $R$ | $R_{L,U} = R^{\exp(\pm z_{0.95} \cdot \text{SE})}$ | log-transformed CI |
| 19 | Weibull shape $\beta$ (graphical) | $\beta = \text{slope of } \ln\!\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-F}\right)$ vs $\ln t$ | dimensionless |
| 20 | Weibull scale $\eta$ (graphical) | $\eta = e^{-\text{intercept}/\beta}$ | time |

**Important Conventions Used in the Table**

- $\Gamma(\cdot)$ denotes the **Gamma function**, e.g., $\Gamma(1)=1$, $\Gamma(1.5)=\sqrt{\pi}/2$, $\Gamma(2)=1$.
- $\Phi(\cdot)$ denotes the **Standard Normal Cumulative Distribution Function** (look up in the table at the back of any engineering statistics text).
- The hazard rate $h(t)$ and failure rate $\lambda(t)$ are used interchangeably; in KTU examination scripts always state your assumed definition.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace turbine blade manufacturers** use Weibull analysis of fatigue data to certify **B1 / B10 life** specifications.
- **Automotive OEM warranty departments** apply Lognormal fitting to field-failure kilometre data, allowing forecast of monthly warranty costs.
- **Power plant operators** apply Exponential / Weibull hazard models to schedule **condition-based maintenance** and **risk-based inspection** (RBI).
- **Semiconductor fabs** use Arrhenius-accelerated life testing to project **10-year field reliability from 1,000-hour laboratory tests**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weibull Parameters from a Probability Plot

The Weibull cumulative distribution function is:

$$ F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} $$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:**

$$ 1 - F(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} $$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm on both sides:**

$$ \ln\!\bigl[1 - F(t)\bigr] = -(t/\eta)^{\beta} $$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $-1$ and take the natural logarithm a second time (this is the "double-log" trick):**

$$ \ln\!\left\{-\ln\!\bigl[1 - F(t)\bigr]\right\} = \beta \ln t - \beta \ln\eta $$

**Step 4 — Define new variables for linearisation:**

$$ Y = \ln\!\left\{-\ln\!\bigl[1 - F(t)\bigr]\right\}, \qquad X = \ln t $$

**Step 5 — Recognise the linear equation $Y = mX + c$:**

$$ m = \beta, \qquad c = -\beta \ln\eta $$

**Step 6 — Estimate the slope $m$ and intercept $c$ by least-squares regression of $(X_i, Y_i)$ pairs, then recover the Weibull parameters:**

$$ \hat{\beta} = m, \qquad \hat{\eta} = e^{-c/m} $$

**Engineering Interpretation**

- A slope $\hat{\beta} > 1$ indicates **wear-out** (hazard increases with time — typical of bearings, fatigue, dies).
- A slope $\hat{\beta} = 1$ reduces the Weibull model to the **Exponential** distribution.
- A slope $\hat{\beta} < 1$ indicates **infant mortality** (hazard decreases with time — typical of early software bugs or manufacturing defects that surface quickly).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Likelihood Estimates for the Weibull Distribution (Complete Data)

Consider $n$ independent, identically distributed (i.i.d.) failure times $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$ with Weibull pdf:

$$ f(t_i) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t_i/\eta)^{\beta}} $$

**Step 1 — Write the likelihood function as the product of individual pdfs:**

$$ L(\beta, \eta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t_i/\eta)^{\beta}} $$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood $\ln L$:**

$$ \ln L = n \ln\beta - n\beta \ln\eta + (\beta - 1)\sum_{i=1}^{n}\ln t_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta} $$

**Step 3 — Partial derivative w.r.t. $\eta$, set to zero for MLE of $\eta$:**

$$ \frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \eta} = -\frac{n\beta}{\eta} + \frac{\beta}{\eta^{\beta + 1}}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i^{\beta} = 0 $$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\hat{\eta}$:**

$$ \hat{\eta} = \left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i^{\hat{\beta}}\right)^{1/\hat{\beta}} $$

**Step 5 — Partial derivative w.r.t. $\beta$, set to zero:**

$$ \frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \beta} = \frac{n}{\beta} - n \ln\eta + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\ln t_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\ln t_i + \frac{\ln\eta}{\eta^{\beta}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}t_i^{\beta} = 0 $$

**Step 6 — Iterative Solution:**

No closed-form expression exists for $\hat{\beta}$; **Newton–Raphson iteration** (or commercial solvers, e.g., ReliaSoft Weibull++) is required. The student must show:

$$ \hat{\beta}_{k+1} = \hat{\beta}_{k} - \left[\frac{\partial^{2}\ln L}{\partial\beta^{2}}\right]_{\beta_k}^{-1} \cdot \left[\frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \beta}\right]_{\beta_k} $$

### 3.3 Kaplan–Meier Product-Limit Estimator (with Suspensions)

Let there be $r$ distinct failure times $t_{(1)} < t_{(2)} < \dots < t_{(r)}$ and let $n_i$ denote the *number at risk* immediately before $t_{(i)}$, and $d_i$ the number of failures at $t_{(i)}$.

**Step 1 — The conditional probability of surviving past $t_{(i)}$ given survival up to $t_{(i)}^{-}$:**

$$ \hat{p}_i = 1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i} $$

**Step 2 — The Kaplan–Meier estimator multiplies the conditional survival probabilities up to time $t$:**

$$ \hat{R}(t) = \prod_{i : t_{(i)} \le t}\left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right) $$

**Step 3 — Greenwood's formula for the variance of $\hat{R}(t)$ (used for confidence intervals):**

$$ \widehat{\text{Var}}\!\bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr] = \bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]^{2} \sum_{i : t_{(i)} \le t} \frac{d_i}{n_i(n_i - d_i)} $$

**Step 4 — The two-sided $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $R(t)$ on the log–log scale:**

$$ R_L(t) = \bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]^{\exp\!\left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \widehat{\text{SE}}}{\ln\hat{R}(t)}\right)} $$

$$ R_U(t) = \bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]^{\exp\!\left(\frac{-z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \widehat{\text{SE}}}{\ln\hat{R}(t)}\right)} $$

where $\widehat{\text{SE}} = \sqrt{\widehat{\text{Var}}\!\bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]}/\hat{R}(t)$ and $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the standard normal quantile (1.645 for 90% CI, 1.96 for 95% CI).

### 3.4 Worked Example — Median Rank / Weibull Plot from $n=8$ Failures

Suppose eight bearings are tested to failure with times (in hours):

$$ t = \{ 1200, 1850, 2300, 2700, 3400, 4200, 5500, 7100 \} $$

**Step 1 — Compute the median rank $F_i = (i - 0.3)/(n + 0.4) = (i - 0.3)/8.4$ for $i = 1, \dots, 8$:**

| $i$ | $t_i$ (h) | $F_i$ | $Y_i = \ln[-\ln(1-F_i)]$ | $X_i = \ln t_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1200 | 0.0833 | -2.418 | 7.090 |
| 2 | 1850 | 0.2024 | -1.412 | 7.523 |
| 3 | 2300 | 0.3214 | -0.812 | 7.740 |
| 4 | 2700 | 0.4405 | -0.366 | 7.901 |
| 5 | 3400 | 0.5595 | +0.0004 | 8.131 |
| 6 | 4200 | 0.6786 | +0.360 | 8.343 |
| 7 | 5500 | 0.7976 | +0.738 | 8.613 |
| 8 | 7100 | 0.9167 | +1.190 | 8.868 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression $Y = mX + c$ on the eight $(X_i, Y_i)$ pairs:**

$$ \bar{X} = 8.026, \qquad \bar{Y} = -0.340 $$

$$ S_{xx} = \sum (X_i - \bar X)^2 = 2.260, \qquad S_{xy} = \sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y) = 5.044 $$

$$ m = S_{xy}/S_{xx} = 5.044/2.260 = 2.232, \qquad c = \bar Y - m\bar X = -0.340 - (2.232)(8.026) = -18.249 $$

**Step 3 — Recover the Weibull parameters:**

$$ \hat{\beta} = m = 2.23, \qquad \hat{\eta} = e^{-c/m} = e^{18.249/2.232} = e^{8.176} \approx 3557 \text{ h} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the B10 life (time at which 10% of the population has failed, i.e., $F(t) = 0.10$):**

$$ 0.10 = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} \;\Rightarrow\; (t/\eta)^{\beta} = -\ln(0.90) = 0.10536 $$

$$ t = \eta (0.10536)^{1/\beta} = 3557 \times (0.10536)^{1/2.23} = 3557 \times 0.3854 = 1371 \text{ h} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the MTTF (numerical value using the gamma function):**

$$ MTTF = \eta \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta) = 3557 \times \Gamma(1.448) $$

Since $\Gamma(1.448) \approx 0.886$ (from gamma tables),

$$ MTTF \approx 3557 \times 0.886 \approx 3152 \text{ h} $$

**Engineering Conclusion:** With $\hat{\beta} = 2.23 > 1$, the bearings exhibit *clear wear-out* behaviour. The plant should schedule preventive replacement at or before the **B10 life of 1,371 h** to maintain high fleet reliability.

### 3.5 Acceleration Factor — Numerical Application

A chip is rated for 10 years at 55 °C. Accelerated testing is performed at 125 °C. Given $E_a = 0.7$ eV and $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K:

$$ T_u = 55 + 273.15 = 328.15 \text{ K}, \qquad T_a = 125 + 273.15 = 398.15 \text{ K} $$

$$ AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{0.7}{8.617 \times 10^{-5}}\left(\frac{1}{328.15} - \frac{1}{398.15}\right)\right] $$

$$ \frac{1}{328.15} - \frac{1}{398.15} = 0.003048 - 0.002512 = 5.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1} $$

$$ AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{0.7 \times 5.36 \times 10^{-4}}{8.617 \times 10^{-5}}\right] = \exp(4.355) \approx 77.9 $$

**Interpretation:** 1 hour at 125 °C is equivalent to 77.9 hours at 55 °C. To demonstrate 10 years = 87,600 h of field life, an accelerated test of only $87{,}600 / 77.9 \approx 1{,}124$ h is sufficient (≈ 47 days).

### 3.6 Analytic Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use

| Question in Hand | Recommended Tool | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| All units failed, $n$ small, no distribution assumed | **Median rank + probability paper** | Simple, visually transparent |
| All units failed, $n \ge 20$, wear-out suspected | **Weibull MLE** | Asymptotic efficiency, supports CI |
| Many suspensions, point-failure data | **Kaplan–Meier** | Uses the censored information |
| Interval inspection data, grouped failures | **Actuarial method** | Designed for grouped/censored bins |
| Test under multiple stress levels | **Arrhenius + Weibull** | Combines physics with statistics |
| Confidence intervals required on $R(t)$ | **MLE + Fisher Information Matrix** | Yields both point and interval estimates |
| Comparing two designs | **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)** | Rigorous hypothesis testing |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Failure Data Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid block illustrates the canonical pipeline a KTU student must mentally internalise for any numerical problem involving field or test data.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Failure Observations] --> A1[Data Cleaning and Validation]
    A1 --> A2{All Units Failed?}
    A2 -- Yes --> A3[Sort and Rank Failures]
    A2 -- No --> A4[Flag Censored Observations]
    A3 --> A5[Compute Plotting Position Fi]
    A4 --> A6[Compute Risk Set Ni]
    A5 --> A7[Probability Paper Plot]
    A6 --> A8[Kaplan Meier or Actuarial Estimate]
    A7 --> A9{Linear Fit Acceptable?}
    A8 --> A10[Step Function R hat of t]
    A9 -- Yes --> A11[Extract Beta and Eta from Slope and Intercept]

    A15 --> A16
    A16 --> A17[Final Reliability Statement and Report]
```

### 4.3 Weibull Probability Plot Schematic (Schematic Description)

The Weibull probability paper is constructed so that the X-axis is logarithmic in time and the Y-axis is the *double-log* transformed cumulative failure probability. On this special paper, a Weibull distribution plots as a *straight line* — slope $= \beta$, intercept $= -\beta \ln \eta$.

| Plot Element | Meaning on Weibull Paper |
|---|---|
| **X-axis label** | $\ln t$ (natural log of time) |
| **Y-axis label** | $\ln[-\ln(1-F)]$ (double-log transform) |
| **Slope of the line** | Weibull shape parameter $\beta$ |
| **Y-intercept (extrapolated to $t=1$)** | $-\beta \ln\eta$ |
| **Point at $F=0.632$** | The characteristic life $\eta$ |
| **Point at $F=0.10$** | B10 life (read on X-axis) |

A non-linear pattern on this paper is the diagnostic signature of *the wrong distribution has been chosen*. A concave-up curve suggests Lognormal; a concave-down curve suggests that the data may contain a mixture of two failure modes (a "dogleg" plot).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – April 2024]**

**Q1. Distinguish between complete and right-censored failure data, with one industrial example of each. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Complete data:** All $n$ test units have experienced failure by the end of the test; exact failure times $t_1, \dots, t_n$ are known. *Example:* life-testing 25 incandescent bulbs in a laboratory until all bulbs burn out.
- **Right-censored data:** At the conclusion of the test, $c$ units out of $n$ are still operational; their *exact failure time is unknown*, but we know each survived at least to the suspension time $s_i$. *Example:* 100 industrial pumps on field test for 6 months, of which 14 are still running at 6 months — their failure times are right-censored at 4,320 h.
- The key mathematical implication: in complete data the *exact contribution* $f(t_i)$ to the likelihood is used; in censored data the *survival probability* $R(s_i)$ is used in its place.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. State and briefly justify the Median Rank formula used in failure-data plotting. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Median Rank** plotting position for the $i$-th ordered failure in a sample of size $n$ is:

$$ F_i^{MdR} = \frac{i - 0.3}{n + 0.4} $$

It is justified because it is the **median of the distribution of $F(t_i)$** (i.e., the value $p$ for which the probability that the true $F(t_i)$ is less than $p$ equals 0.5). It is preferred over the mean rank $i/(n+1)$ for small sample sizes ($n < 30$) because it has **lower bias** in estimating the population CDF, and it corresponds exactly to the median of the **Beta distribution** that governs the order statistics of a uniform sample. It is universally used in commercial software such as ReliaSoft Weibull++.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Module 4, Question A]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Define the Weibull distribution. Derive the relationship between its CDF, reliability function, hazard rate, and shape parameter. Discuss the physical interpretation of $\beta < 1$, $\beta = 1$, and $\beta > 1$.* **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** *The following failure times (in hours) were observed for 8 identical components tested to failure: $\{1500, 2200, 2900, 3500, 4100, 5000, 6400, 8200\}$. Using the Median Rank method, determine the Weibull parameters $\hat{\beta}$ and $\hat{\eta}$ by linear regression on the Weibull probability paper. Also estimate the B10 life and the MTTF. [Given $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.889$.]* **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The Weibull CDF is defined as:

$$ F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \ge 0, \quad \beta, \eta > 0 $$

**Derivation of the Reliability Function:**

$$ R(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} $$

**Derivation of the PDF by differentiating the CDF:**

$$ f(t) = \frac{dF}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} $$

**Derivation of the Hazard Rate:**

$$ h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} $$

**Physical Interpretation of $\beta$:**

- $\beta < 1$: $h(t)$ is **decreasing** → *infant-mortality regime* (defects manifest early; surviving population becomes more reliable with time). Example: solder-joint defects, software bugs.
- $\beta = 1$: $h(t) = \lambda$ is **constant** → Weibull degenerates into the **Exponential distribution**; the system is *memoryless*. Example: random electronic noise failures.
- $\beta > 1$: $h(t)$ is **increasing** → *wear-out regime* (degradation mechanisms dominate). Example: bearing fatigue, filament evaporation, corrosion.

**Significance of $\eta$:** The *characteristic life* is the time at which 63.2% of the population has failed, because $F(\eta) = 1 - e^{-1} = 0.632$.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the data, compute Median Rank and double-log transform:**

| $i$ | $t_i$ (h) | $F_i$ (MdR) | $Y_i = \ln[-\ln(1-F_i)]$ | $X_i = \ln t_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1500 | 0.0833 | -2.418 | 7.313 |
| 2 | 2200 | 0.2024 | -1.412 | 7.696 |
| 3 | 2900 | 0.3214 | -0.812 | 7.973 |
| 4 | 3500 | 0.4405 | -0.366 | 8.161 |
| 5 | 4100 | 0.5595 | +0.0004 | 8.319 |
| 6 | 5000 | 0.6786 | +0.360 | 8.517 |
| 7 | 6400 | 0.7976 | +0.738 | 8.764 |
| 8 | 8200 | 0.9167 | +1.190 | 9.011 |

**Step 2 — Compute regression statistics:**

$$ \bar X = 8.219, \quad \bar Y = -0.340 $$

$$ S_{xx} = \sum (X_i - \bar X)^2 = 2.328, \quad S_{xy} = \sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y) = 5.150 $$

$$ m = S_{xy}/S_{xx} = 5.150 / 2.328 = 2.213, \quad c = \bar Y - m\bar X = -0.340 - (2.213)(8.219) = -18.527 $$

**Step 3 — Recover the Weibull parameters:**

$$ \hat{\beta} = 2.21, \qquad \hat{\eta} = e^{-c/m} = e^{18.527/2.213} = e^{8.373} \approx 4{,}360 \text{ h} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the B10 life:**

$$ (t/\eta)^{\beta} = -\ln(0.90) = 0.10536 $$

$$ t_{B10} = 4360 \times (0.10536)^{1/2.21} = 4360 \times 0.3841 = 1{,}675 \text{ h} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the MTTF:**

$$ MTTF = \eta \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta) = 4360 \times \Gamma(1.452) $$

Using the hint $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.889$ and $\Gamma(1.452) \approx 0.886$:

$$ MTTF \approx 4360 \times 0.886 \approx 3{,}863 \text{ h} $$

**Conclusion:** $\beta = 2.21 > 1$ confirms wear-out behaviour. The plant should plan preventive replacement at or before the **B10 life of 1,675 h**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Module 4, Question B (Alternative Choice)]**

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Define the Kaplan–Meier estimator. For a small dataset containing both failures and suspensions, explain the steps to compute the estimator. State the Greenwood formula for the variance and its role in constructing confidence intervals.* **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** *Ten identical components are placed on life test. Failures occur at the following times (h): 320, 540, 880, 1400, 2100, 3000. Three components are still operating at the test termination time of 3000 h. Compute the Kaplan–Meier estimate of $R(t)$ at $t = 1500$ h and at $t = 3000$ h, and report the 90% confidence interval at $t = 1500$ h. Use $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.* **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The Kaplan–Meier (product-limit) estimator $\hat{R}(t)$ is a *non-parametric* step function that estimates the survival function from right-censored data. It is *the* most widely used estimator in medical statistics, survival analysis, and modern reliability engineering.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$ \hat{R}(t) = \prod_{i \,:\, t_{(i)} \le t}\left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right) $$

where $d_i$ is the number of failures at $t_{(i)}$ and $n_i$ is the *risk set* (number of components operating just before $t_{(i)}$).

**Computational Steps:**

1. Sort the observed times (failures and suspensions) in ascending order.
2. At each distinct failure time $t_{(i)}$, compute the risk set $n_i$ (subtract any prior failures and suspensions).
3. Compute the conditional failure probability $d_i/n_i$ and survival $1 - d_i/n_i$.
4. Multiply successive survival probabilities up to time $t$.

**Greenwood's Formula for the Variance:**

$$ \widehat{\text{Var}}\!\bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr] = \bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]^2 \sum_{i \,:\, t_{(i)} \le t} \frac{d_i}{n_i(n_i - d_i)} $$

This variance estimate is then used to construct a two-sided $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ CI:

$$ R_L(t) = \bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]^{\exp\!\left(+z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma_G / \ln\hat{R}(t)\right)} $$

$$ R_U(t) = \bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]^{\exp\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma_G / \ln\hat{R}(t)\right)} $$

where $\sigma_G = \sqrt{\widehat{\text{Var}}[\hat{R}(t)]}/\hat{R}(t)$. The log-transformation ensures the CI lies in $[0,1]$ and is more accurate for small samples.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the ordered observations (F = failure, S = suspension at 3000 h):**

| Order | $t$ (h) | Type | $d_i$ | $n_i$ | $1 - d_i/n_i$ | $\hat{R}(t)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 320 | F | 1 | 10 | 0.900 | 0.9000 |
| 2 | 540 | F | 1 | 9 | 0.8889 | 0.8000 |
| 3 | 880 | F | 1 | 8 | 0.8750 | 0.7000 |
| 4 | 1400 | F | 1 | 7 | 0.8571 | 0.6000 |
| 5 | 2100 | F | 1 | 6 | 0.8333 | 0.5000 |
| 6 | 3000 | F | 1 | 5 | 0.8000 | 0.4000 |
| 7 | 3000 | S | 0 | 4 | 1.000 | 0.4000 |
| 8 | 3000 | S | 0 | 3 | 1.000 | 0.4000 |
| 9 | 3000 | S | 0 | 2 | 1.000 | 0.4000 |

**Step 2 — Read off $\hat{R}(t)$ at the requested times:**

- At $t = 1500$ h (between the 4th and 5th failures): $\hat{R}(1500) = 0.6000$.
- At $t = 3000$ h (just after the 6th failure): $\hat{R}(3000) = 0.4000$.

**Step 3 — Compute Greenwood's variance at $t = 1500$ h:**

$$ \widehat{\text{Var}}[\hat{R}(1500)] = (0.6)^2 \left[\frac{1}{10 \cdot 9} + \frac{1}{9 \cdot 8} + \frac{1}{8 \cdot 7} + \frac{1}{7 \cdot 6}\right] $$

$$ = 0.36 \left[0.01111 + 0.01389 + 0.01786 + 0.02381\right] = 0.36 \times 0.06667 = 0.02400 $$

$$ \sigma_G = \frac{\sqrt{0.02400}}{0.6} = \frac{0.15492}{0.6} = 0.25820 $$

**Step 4 — Construct the 90% CI using $z_{0.05} = 1.645$:**

$$ \exp\!\left(\pm \frac{1.645 \times 0.25820}{\ln 0.6}\right) = \exp\!\left(\mp \frac{0.4247}{0.5108}\right) = \exp(\mp 0.8315) $$

$$ e^{-0.8315} = 0.4354, \qquad e^{+0.8315} = 2.297 $$

$$ R_L(1500) = 0.6^{2.297} = 0.321, \qquad R_U(1500) = 0.6^{0.4354} = 0.811 $$

**Result:** The 90% CI for $R(1500)$ is approximately $[0.321,\; 0.811]$. The point estimate of 0.60 is consistent with the failure of 4 out of 10 components in the first 1,500 h.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks in PERUT636 Module 4 — Failure Data Analysis**
> 1. **Confusing Mean Rank with Median Rank:** The Mean Rank $i/(n+1)$ over-estimates $F(t_i)$ for small $i$ and under-estimates for large $i$. KTU examiners expect the **Median Rank** unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 2. **Forgetting the censoring adjustment in the risk set $n_i$:** If a component is suspended at time $t_s$, it must be subtracted from the risk set of *every* subsequent failure.
> 3. **Reporting $\hat{\eta}$ without units:** $\eta$ is always a *time*; the unit (h, cycles, km) must be explicitly stated.
> 4. **Skipping the linearity check on the Weibull plot:** A "fitted" $\beta$ from a clearly non-linear plot is invalid; the examiner expects a note such as *"R² = 0.987, indicating acceptable Weibull fit."*
> 5. **Using Mean instead of Median for $R(t)$ confidence interval construction:** The **log-transformation of Greenwood's standard error** is *mandatory* to keep the CI inside $[0,1]$; the simple Wald interval $\hat{R} \pm 1.645 \cdot \text{SE}$ can produce nonsensical negative upper bounds.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (15 high-yield points)**
> 1. Failure data can be **complete, right-censored, interval-censored, or left-censored**; each demands a different statistical tool.
> 2. The **Weibull CDF** $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ is the *workhorse* of reliability engineering; $\beta$ = shape, $\eta$ = scale.
> 3. $\beta < 1$ → infant mortality; $\beta = 1$ → exponential; $\beta > 1$ → wear-out.
> 4. The **Median Rank** $F_i = (i-0.3)/(n+0.4)$ is the default plotting position for $n<30$.
> 5. Linearising transform: $Y = \ln[-\ln(1-F)]$, $X = \ln t$. Slope $= \beta$, intercept $= -\beta \ln \eta$.
> 6. The **characteristic life** $\eta$ corresponds to $F = 0.632$, *not* to the mean.
> 7. **MLE for Weibull** has no closed form for $\beta$; Newton–Raphson iteration is the standard solver.
> 8. **MTTF (Weibull)** $= \eta \Gamma(1+1/\beta)$; numerical evaluation of $\Gamma$ is essential.
> 9. **B10 life** $= \eta \cdot [-\ln 0.9]^{1/\beta}$ is the plant-maintenance language; **L10** is the classical bearing spec.
> 10. **Kaplan–Meier** is the gold standard for **right-censored** data; it produces a *step function* $\hat{R}(t)$.
> 11. **Greenwood's variance** enables confidence intervals on $\hat{R}(t)$ via the log-transformation.
> 12. The **Arrhenius equation** converts accelerated-test hours to field hours using the Boltzmann constant $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K.
> 13. **Hazard rate** $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$; it is *not* a probability — its units are $1/\text{time}$.
> 14. For **interval/grouped data**, use the **Actuarial method** (also called the life-table method).
> 15. Always close an FDA report with a **goodness-of-fit test** (Anderson–Darling is preferred) and a statement of the practical engineering implication (e.g., "set preventive replacement at B10").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Prediction Based on Weibull Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reliability Prediction Based on Weibull Distribution ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Weibull Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution extensively used in reliability engineering, life data analysis, and failure prediction. It is mathematically defined by a *shape parameter* ($\beta$) and a *scale parameter* ($\eta$), and optionally a *location parameter* ($\gamma$). In reliability engineering, the Weibull distribution is favored because its **Hazard Function (Failure Rate)** can take on a wide variety of shapes — making it uniquely capable of modeling the entire **Bathtub Curve** of product life (infant mortality, useful life, and wear-out phases). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Weibull distribution is the most versatile life distribution model used for reliability prediction. It provides the probability of failure of a component as a function of time, given by the cumulative distribution function:* > *F(t) = 1 − exp[−(t/η)$\beta$], for t ≥ 0* > *where β > 0 is the shape parameter, η > 0 is the scale parameter (characteristic life).* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a fleet of identical bulbs installed across a city at the same instant. You sit with a notebook and record when each bulb fails. When you plot these failure times on a graph, you notice three distinct behaviors: 1. **Early Failures:** Some bulbs die very quickly due to manufacturing defects (infant mortality). 2. **Mid-Life Failures:** A large, steady percentage of bulbs fail uniformly — the randomness of accidents, voltage spikes, etc. 3. **Late Failures:** The remaining bulbs fail in rapid succession near their end-of-life due to filament aging (wear-out). A single mathematical curve that captures **all three** behaviors simultaneously is the **Weibull Distribution**. The genius of Weibull lies in its **shape parameter ($\beta$)**: - If you squeeze the curve to the left (many early failures), $\beta < 1$. - If you stretch it out evenly (constant rate), $\beta = 1$. - If you push it to the right (wear-out), $\beta > 1$. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English:** The Weibull distribution is the "Swiss Army Knife" of reliability prediction — one flexible equation that fits almost any real-world failure pattern you will encounter in industrial practice. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Symbol | Parameter Name | Physical Meaning | Valid Range | |:------:|:--------------:|:----------------|:-----------:| | $\beta$ | **Shape Parameter** | Controls the *failure behavior pattern* | $\beta > 0$ | | $\eta$ | **Scale Parameter** | **Characteristic Life** (63.2% of units fail by $t = \eta$) | $\eta > 0$ | | $\gamma$ | **Location Parameter** | Shift of the distribution along time axis | $\gamma \geq 0$ | | $t$ | Time | Operating time of the component | $t \geq 0$ | | $R(t)$ | Reliability Function | Probability of survival beyond time $t$ | $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Highlight:** In the majority of KTU exam problems, the location parameter $\gamma = 0$, giving us the **2-parameter Weibull Distribution**. Always state this assumption explicitly in your answer scripts. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Formulation — The Five Core Functions The Weibull distribution is fully described by **five interconnected functions**. Mastering these five is the single most important task for scoring well in KTU exams on this topic. **Step 1 — Probability Density Function (PDF):** The PDF describes the *relative likelihood* of failure at any specific instant of time. $$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):** The CDF gives the *probability that a unit has failed by time $t$* — this is the unreliability. $$F(t) = 1 - \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Reliability Function (Survival Function):** The reliability is the probability of *surviving* past time $t$. $$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ **Step 4 — Hazard Function (Instantaneous Failure Rate):** The hazard rate quantifies the *conditional probability of failure in the next small time interval*, given survival up to time $t$. $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$ **Step 5 — Cumulative Hazard Function:** The cumulative hazard is the integral of the hazard rate and is useful for accelerated life testing. $$H(t) = \int_0^t h(\tau)\, d\tau = \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$ ### 2.2 Physical Interpretation of the Shape Parameter $\beta$ The shape parameter $\beta$ is the single most diagnostic number in reliability engineering. It tells you exactly *which phase of the bathtub curve* your component is currently in: - **$\beta < 1$ → Decreasing Failure Rate (Infant Mortality):** Most failures occur early. The product has manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, or quality issues. Reliability *improves* with time as weak units are weeded out. - **$\beta = 1$ → Constant Failure Rate (Useful Life / Random Failures):** The distribution reduces to the **Exponential Distribution**. Failures occur randomly and independently — characteristic of complex electronic systems in their useful life. - **$\beta > 1$ → Increasing Failure Rate (Wear-Out):** Failures accelerate with age. Typical of mechanical components — bearings, gears, filaments, tires. - **$\beta = 2$ → Rayleigh Distribution:** A special case used heavily in vibration analysis and fatigue modeling. - **$\beta \approx 3.4$ → Approximates the Normal Distribution:** Useful when failure times cluster symmetrically around a mean. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When a KTU numerical problem provides failure data and asks you to "identify the type of failure pattern," your answer must explicitly state whether the estimated $\beta$ corresponds to infant mortality, useful life, or wear-out — and recommend an appropriate engineering action (e.g., burn-in for $\beta < 1$, preventive replacement for $\beta > 1$). ### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Function | Formula | Units / Domain | KTU Use Case | |:---------|:--------|:---------------|:-------------| | **PDF** | $f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $f(t) \geq 0$ | Probability density calculation | | **CDF** | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $0 \leq F(t) \leq 1$ | Cumulative failure probability | | **Reliability** | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ | Survival probability | | **Hazard Rate** | $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | failures / unit time | Instantaneous failure rate | | **Cum. Hazard** | $H(t) = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$ | dimensionless | Maintenance scheduling | | **Mean Life (MTTF)** | $E(T) = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | time units | Expected operational life | | **Median Life** | $t_{0.5} = \eta \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$ | time units | 50% failure time | | **Characteristic Life** | $t_{0.632} = \eta$ | time units | 63.2% failure time | | **Mode** | $\eta \left(\frac{\beta - 1}{\beta}\right)^{1/\beta}$ for $\beta > 1$ | time units | Most likely failure time | | **Variance** | $\text{Var}(T) = \eta^2 \left[\Gamma\left(1+\tfrac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^2\left(1+\tfrac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | time² units | Spread of failure times | | **Gamma values** | $\Gamma(1) = 1$; $\Gamma(1.5) = 0.8862$; $\Gamma(2) = 1$; $\Gamma(2.5) = 1.3293$ | dimensionless | Required for MTTF calcs | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Weibull distribution has become the **industry standard** for reliability prediction because of its unmatched flexibility: - **Aerospace:** Predicting fatigue life of turbine blades and airframe structures. - **Electronics:** Modeling the lifetime of capacitors, semiconductor devices, and solder joints. - **Automotive:** Warranty analysis of components like brake pads, batteries, and bearings. - **Manufacturing:** Identifying which products need burn-in testing (when $\beta < 1$). - **Maintenance Engineering:** Deciding optimal preventive replacement intervals (when $\beta > 1$). - **Quality Control:** Setting acceptable failure thresholds for Six Sigma reliability programs. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving the Reliability Function from the CDF **Starting Premise:** The Weibull CDF is defined as: $$F(t) = 1 - \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ **Step 1 — Apply the Fundamental Axiom of Probability:** By definition, the reliability function is the complement of the cumulative distribution function: $$R(t) + F(t) = 1$$ **Step 2 — Isolate R(t):** Rearranging the equation above: $$R(t) = 1 - F(t)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the Weibull CDF:** Direct substitution of the given CDF expression: $$R(t) = 1 - \left\{1 - \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]\right\}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify Algebraically:** The two 1's cancel out, leaving: $$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]}$$ This is the cornerstone equation used to answer virtually every "find the probability of survival" question in KTU exams. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Deriving the Hazard Function $h(t)$ **Step 1 — Start with the General Definition:** The hazard rate is universally defined as the ratio of the PDF to the reliability function: $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Weibull PDF:** Insert $f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ into the numerator: $$h(t) = \frac{\frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}{R(t)}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the Weibull Reliability Function:** Insert $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ into the denominator: $$h(t) = \frac{\frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}{e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}$$ **Step 4 — Cancel the Exponential Terms:** Since the numerator and denominator both contain $e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$, they cancel perfectly: $$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$ **Step 5 — Final Simplified Form:** $$\boxed{h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta^{\beta}} \, t^{\beta - 1}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** Note that the exponential term completely disappears in the hazard function! This is a remarkable property of the Weibull distribution that makes it mathematically tractable — the hazard rate is a pure power-law function of time. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mean Time to Failure (MTTF) Using the Gamma Function **Step 1 — General Definition of Expected Value:** For any continuous random variable with PDF $f(t)$, the expected value is: $$E(T) = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Weibull PDF:** $$E(T) = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} \, dt$$ **Step 3 — Apply Change of Variable:** Let $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$, which gives $t = \eta \cdot u^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = \frac{\eta}{\beta} u^{1/\beta - 1} du$. $$E(T) = \int_0^{\infty} \eta u^{1/\beta} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta} \cdot u^{(\beta-1)/\beta} \cdot e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\eta}{\beta} u^{1/\beta - 1} \, du$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the Exponents:** Combining all exponents of $u$: $1/\beta + (\beta-1)/\beta + 1/\beta - 1 = (1 + \beta - 1 + 1)/\beta - 1 = (1 + 1/\beta) - 1 = 1/\beta - 1 + 1 = 1/\beta + ... $ After full simplification: $$E(T) = \eta \int_0^{\infty} u^{1/\beta} \, e^{-u} \, du$$ **Step 5 — Recognize the Gamma Function:** By definition, $\Gamma(k) = \int_0^{\infty} u^{k-1} e^{-u} du$. With $k = 1 + 1/\beta$: $$\boxed{E(T) = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}$$ **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):** For $\beta = 2$, $\eta = 1000$ hours: $$MTTF = 1000 \cdot \Gamma(1 + 0.5) = 1000 \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = 1000 \cdot 0.8862 = 886.2 \text{ hours}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for Weibull Parameters The MLE method is the most widely used technique for estimating $\beta$ and $\eta$ from sample failure data in KTU exam problems. **Step 1 — Construct the Likelihood Function:** Given $n$ observed failure times $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$, the likelihood of observing this data is: $$L(\beta, \eta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} f(t_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Take the Natural Logarithm (Log-Likelihood):** $$\ln L = n \ln\beta - n\beta \ln\eta + (\beta - 1) \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln t_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$ **Step 3 — Partial Derivative with Respect to $\eta$:** $$\frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \eta} = -\frac{n\beta}{\eta} + \frac{\beta}{\eta^{\beta+1}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i^{\beta} = 0$$ Solving this gives the MLE for $\eta$ as a function of $\beta$: $$\hat{\eta}^{\beta} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i^{\beta}}{n}$$ **Step 4 — Partial Derivative with Respect to $\beta$:** $$\frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \beta} = \frac{n}{\beta} - n \ln\eta + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln t_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta} \ln\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right) = 0$$ **Step 5 — The Implicit Equation for $\hat{\beta}$:** This transcendental equation must be solved **iteratively** (Newton-Raphson method) since no closed-form solution exists: $$\frac{n}{\beta} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln t_i - n \cdot \frac{\sum t_i^{\beta} \ln t_i}{\sum t_i^{\beta}} = 0$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students try to write a "clean" formula for $\hat{\beta}$. There isn't one — it always requires **iterative numerical computation**. In your exam, describe the iterative procedure clearly and show one or two iterations to demonstrate understanding. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5: The Weibull Linearization (Probability Plot Method) This graphical method is extremely popular in KTU numerical problems because it converts the Weibull CDF into a straight line on suitable paper. **Step 1 — Start with the CDF:** $$F(t) = 1 - \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to Isolate the Exponential:** $$1 - F(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Take the Natural Logarithm Twice (Double Log Transformation):** First log: $-\ln[1 - F(t)] = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$ Second log: $\ln\{-\ln[1 - F(t)]\} = \beta \ln t - \beta \ln\eta$ **Step 4 — Identify the Linear Form:** Define $y = \ln\{-\ln[1 - F(t)]\}$ and $x = \ln t$. Then: $$y = \beta \cdot x - \beta \ln\eta$$ This is the equation of a **straight line** with slope $\beta$ and intercept $-\beta \ln\eta$. **Step 5 — Estimate Parameters from the Linear Plot:** - **Shape parameter:** $\hat{\beta}$ = slope of the fitted line - **Scale parameter:** $\hat{\eta} = \exp(-\text{intercept} / \hat{\beta})$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Weibull Reliability Prediction Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Collect Failure Data
ti values from tests] --> B[Select Distribution Family
Weibull Assumption] B --> C[Choose Estimation Method
MLE or Graphical] C --> D{Method Type?} D -- MLE --> E[Build Log-Likelihood Function] D -- Graphical --> F[Weibull Probability Paper Plot] E --> G[Iteratively Solve for Beta] F --> H[Find Slope and Intercept] G --> I[Compute Eta from Beta] H --> I I --> J[Validate Fit
Goodness-of-Fit Tests] J --> K{Fit Acceptable?} K -- Yes --> L[Compute Reliability R of t] K -- No --> M[Reconsider Distribution Choice] L --> N[Compute MTTF and Hazard Rate] N --> O[Generate Reliability Prediction Report] O --> P[Engineering Decisions
Maintenance / Replacement] ``` ### 4.2 Weibull Shape Parameter Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Estimated Beta Value] --> B{Beta Comparison} B -- Beta less than 1 --> C[Infant Mortality Phase
Decreasing Failure Rate] B -- Beta equals 1 --> D[Useful Life Phase
Constant Failure Rate
Exponential Distribution] B -- Beta between 1 and 3 --> E[Wear-Out Phase
Slowly Increasing] B -- Beta between 3 and 4 --> F[Wear-Out Phase
Rapidly Increasing] B -- Beta equals 2 --> G[Rayleigh Special Case] B -- Beta near 3.4 --> H[Approximates Normal Distribution] C --> C1[Action: Burn-In Testing] D --> D1[Action: Corrective Maintenance] E --> E1[Action: Scheduled Replacement] F --> F1[Action: Aggressive Preventive Replacement] G --> G1[Action: Vibration Analysis] H --> H1[Action: Statistical Quality Control] ``` ### 4.3 Weibull Function Hierarchy Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Weibull Distribution Family] --> B[CDF F of t] A --> C[PDF f of t] A --> D[Reliability R of t] A --> E[Hazard h of t] A --> F[Cumulative Hazard H of t] B --> G[Unreliability Measure] C --> H[Failure Density] D --> I[Survival Probability] E --> J[Instantaneous Risk] F --> K[Total Accumulated Risk] G --> L[Maintenance Decision Support] H --> L I --> L J --> L K --> L ``` ### 4.4 Parameter Estimation Methodology Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Parameter Estimation Methods] --> B[Method of Moments] A --> C[Maximum Likelihood Estimation] A --> D[Graphical Method] A --> E[Least Squares Regression] B --> B1[Simple but Less Accurate] C --> C1[Most Accurate
Requires Iteration] D --> D1[Visual Intuition
Probability Paper] E --> E1[Computational Efficiency
Linear Regression] B1 --> F[Best for Quick Estimates] C1 --> G[Best for Final Analysis] D1 --> H[Best for Engineering Presentations] E1 --> I[Best for Software Implementation] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** State the reliability function of the Weibull distribution and explain the physical significance of the shape parameter ($\beta$) and the scale parameter ($\eta$). **Model Answer:** The Weibull reliability function is: $$R(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ - **Shape parameter ($\beta$):** Determines the failure pattern. $\beta < 1$ indicates infant mortality, $\beta = 1$ indicates constant failure rate (exponential case), and $\beta > 1$ indicates wear-out. - **Scale parameter ($\eta$):** Known as the *characteristic life*. It is the time at which **63.2% of the population has failed** (since $R(\eta) = e^{-1} = 0.368$). It stretches or compresses the distribution along the time axis without altering its shape. *(3 marks: 1 mark for formula + 2 marks for parameter interpretation)* --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** A component follows a Weibull distribution with $\beta = 2.5$ and $\eta = 5000$ hours. Calculate the reliability at $t = 3000$ hours. **Model Answer:** Given: $\beta = 2.5$, $\eta = 5000$ hours, $t = 3000$ hours. $$R(3000) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{3000}{5000}\right)^{2.5}\right]$$ $$R(3000) = \exp\left[-(0.6)^{2.5}\right]$$ $$(0.6)^{2.5} = 0.6^2 \cdot 0.6^{0.5} = 0.36 \cdot 0.7746 = 0.2789$$ $$R(3000) = e^{-0.2789} = 0.7568 \approx 75.68\%$$ *(3 marks: 1 mark substitution + 1 mark computation + 1 mark final answer)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Derive the expressions for the PDF, CDF, and Hazard Function of the 2-parameter Weibull distribution. Explain how the value of the shape parameter $\beta$ influences the nature of the failure rate. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The failure times (in hours) of 8 bearings observed during a life test are: 120, 250, 380, 510, 690, 850, 1020, 1200. Using the graphical method (Weibull probability plot), estimate the shape and scale parameters. Calculate the MTTF and the reliability at 800 hours. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Derivation of the CDF:** The Weibull distribution is built on the principle that the cumulative hazard function is a power function of time: $$H(t) = \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$ Since $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$, we obtain: $$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ **Derivation of the PDF:** Differentiating $F(t)$ with respect to $t$: $$f(t) = \frac{dF}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ **Derivation of the Hazard Function:** $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$ **Influence of $\beta$ on Failure Rate:** - $\beta < 1$: $h(t)$ is a decreasing function of $t$ → Infant mortality phase. - $\beta = 1$: $h(t) = 1/\eta$ (constant) → Useful life phase (Exponential). - $\beta > 1$: $h(t)$ is an increasing function of $t$ → Wear-out phase. *(7 marks: 3 marks derivations + 4 marks interpretation)* **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Step 1 — Rank the data and compute median ranks:** | Rank ($i$) | $t_i$ (hours) | Median Rank $F_i = \frac{i - 0.3}{n + 0.4}$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 120 | 0.0833 | | 2 | 250 | 0.1905 | | 3 | 380 | 0.2976 | | 4 | 510 | 0.4048 | | 5 | 690 | 0.5119 | | 6 | 850 | 0.6190 | | 7 | 1020 | 0.7262 | | 8 | 1200 | 0.8333 | **Step 2 — Compute transformed variables:** For each point, calculate $x = \ln(t)$ and $y = \ln\{-\ln(1-F)\}$: | $t_i$ | $F_i$ | $x = \ln t_i$ | $1 - F_i$ | $-\ln(1-F_i)$ | $y = \ln[-\ln(1-F_i)]$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 120 | 0.0833 | 4.787 | 0.9167 | 0.0870 | -2.442 | | 250 | 0.1905 | 5.521 | 0.8095 | 0.2114 | -1.554 | | 380 | 0.2976 | 5.940 | 0.7024 | 0.3533 | -1.040 | | 510 | 0.4048 | 6.234 | 0.5952 | 0.5188 | -0.656 | | 690 | 0.5119 | 6.537 | 0.4881 | 0.7175 | -0.332 | | 850 | 0.6190 | 6.745 | 0.3810 | 0.9650 | -0.0356 | | 1020 | 0.7262 | 6.928 | 0.2738 | 1.2962 | 0.259 | | 1200 | 0.8333 | 7.090 | 0.1667 | 1.7917 | 0.583 | **Step 3 — Linear regression on the $(x, y)$ data:** Using least squares: $$\text{Slope } \hat{\beta} = \frac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$$ Computing the sums: $\sum x = 49.782$, $\sum y = -5.218$, $\sum x^2 = 311.34$, $\sum xy = -33.27$. $$\hat{\beta} = \frac{8(-33.27) - (49.782)(-5.218)}{8(311.34) - (49.782)^2} = \frac{-266.16 + 259.79}{2490.72 - 2478.19} = \frac{-6.37}{12.53} \approx 1.95$$ **Step 4 — Compute intercept:** Intercept $= \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}\bar{x} = (-0.652) - (1.95)(6.222) = -0.652 - 12.13 = -12.78$ **Step 5 — Estimate scale parameter:** $$\hat{\eta} = \exp\left(\frac{-\text{intercept}}{\hat{\beta}}\right) = \exp\left(\frac{12.78}{1.95}\right) = \exp(6.55) \approx 700 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 6 — Calculate MTTF:** For $\beta = 1.95$: $\Gamma(1 + 1/1.95) = \Gamma(1.513) \approx 0.887$ $$MTTF = 700 \times 0.887 = 621 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 7 — Reliability at $t = 800$ hours:** $$R(800) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{800}{700}\right)^{1.95}\right] = \exp\left[-(1.143)^{1.95}\right] = e^{-1.318} \approx 0.268 \text{ or } 26.8\%$$ *(7 marks: distributed across ranking, transformation, regression, parameter estimation, MTTF, and reliability calculation)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive the Mean Time to Failure (MTTF) and Variance expressions for the Weibull distribution in terms of the Gamma function. Given $\beta = 3$ and $\eta = 2000$ hours, compute the MTTF and standard deviation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the Method of Maximum Likelihood Estimation, derive the implicit equations for estimating the Weibull parameters $\beta$ and $\eta$. Discuss why these equations are typically solved iteratively rather than analytically. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Derivation of MTTF:** Starting from $E(T) = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f(t) \, dt$ and using the substitution $u = (t/\eta)^\beta$: $$MTTF = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$ **Derivation of Variance:** $$\text{Var}(T) = E(T^2) - [E(T)]^2$$ Where: $$E(T^2) = \int_0^\infty t^2 f(t) \, dt = \eta^2 \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right)$$ Therefore: $$\text{Var}(T) = \eta^2 \left[\Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^2\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]$$ **Numerical Computation:** Given: $\beta = 3$, $\eta = 2000$ hours. Using standard gamma values: $\Gamma(1 + 1/3) = \Gamma(1.333) \approx 0.8930$ and $\Gamma(1 + 2/3) = \Gamma(1.667) \approx 0.9027$. $$MTTF = 2000 \times 0.8930 = 1786 \text{ hours}$$ $$\text{Var}(T) = (2000)^2 [0.9027 - (0.8930)^2] = 4{,}000{,}000 \times [0.9027 - 0.7974] = 4{,}000{,}000 \times 0.1053 = 421{,}200$$ $$\sigma = \sqrt{421{,}200} \approx 649 \text{ hours}$$ *(7 marks: 3 marks derivation + 4 marks numerical)* **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Log-Likelihood Function:** $$L = \prod_{i=1}^n \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$ $$\ln L = n\ln\beta - n\beta\ln\eta + (\beta-1)\sum\ln t_i - \sum\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$ **Partial Derivatives:** $$\frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \eta} = 0 \implies \hat{\eta}^\beta = \frac{\sum t_i^\beta}{n}$$ $$\frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \beta} = \frac{n}{\beta} + \sum \ln t_i - \frac{n \sum t_i^\beta \ln t_i}{\sum t_i^\beta} = 0$$ **Why Iterative Solution?** The equation for $\hat{\beta}$ contains $\beta$ both inside and outside of transcendental functions (powers and logarithms). No closed-form algebraic solution exists. Iterative methods such as **Newton-Raphson** are required: $$\beta_{k+1} = \beta_k - \frac{g(\beta_k)}{g'(\beta_k)}$$ where $g(\beta) = \frac{n}{\beta} + \sum \ln t_i - \frac{n \sum t_i^\beta \ln t_i}{\sum t_i^\beta}$ and $g'(\beta)$ is its derivative. *(7 marks: 4 marks derivations + 3 marks discussion on iteration)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always carry units (hours, cycles, km) through the calculation and into the final answer. > 2. **Confusing $\beta$ and $\eta$ Roles:** $\beta$ controls *shape* (failure behavior); $\eta$ controls *scale* (timing). Examiners routinely test this distinction. > 3. **Incomplete Derivations:** Skipping intermediate steps in MLE derivation costs 2–3 marks. Show every partial derivative and the substitution step. > 4. **Median Rank Formula:** Use $F_i = (i - 0.3)/(n + 0.4)$ for the graphical method, not $i/n$ — the latter introduces bias at small sample sizes. > 5. **Special Cases:** When $\beta = 1$, the Weibull reduces to exponential — explicitly state this when relevant to earn full marks. > 6. **Gamma Function Values:** Memorize $\Gamma(1.5) = 0.8862$ and $\Gamma(2) = 1$ at minimum. Examiners often provide these but you should be ready. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reliability Prediction Based on Weibull Distribution** - ☐ **Five Core Functions to Memorize:** PDF, CDF, $R(t)$, $h(t)$, $H(t)$. - ☐ **Master the Double-Log Transform:** $\ln\{-\ln[1-F(t)]\} = \beta \ln t - \beta \ln \eta$ — this is the gateway to graphical estimation. - ☐ **Shape Parameter $\beta$ Interpretation:** $<1$ = infant mortality; $=1$ = exponential (useful life); $>1$ = wear-out. - ☐ **Characteristic Life $\eta$:** Always equals the time at which $R(\eta) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ (i.e., 63.2% have failed). - ☐ **MTTF Formula:** $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ — remember the gamma function appears in *both* MTTF and variance. - ☐ **MLE is Iterative:** No closed-form solution for $\hat{\beta}$; use Newton-Raphson or numerical software. - ☐ **Median Rank Approximation:** $F_i = (i - 0.3)/(n + 0.4)$ — preferred over $i/n$ for small samples. - ☐ **Special Cases:** $\beta = 1 \to$ Exponential; $\beta = 2 \to$ Rayleigh; $\beta \approx 3.4 \to$ Normal approximation. - ☐ **Engineering Action Mapping:** Match your $\beta$ estimate to the appropriate reliability engineering intervention (burn-in, corrective maintenance, preventive replacement). - ☐ **Gamma Function Values to Memorize:** $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(1.5) = 0.8862$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$, $\Gamma(2.5) = 1.3293$. - ☐ **Common KTU Problem Pattern:** Given failure data → estimate $\beta$, $\eta$ → compute $R(t)$, MTTF, or hazard rate → recommend maintenance strategy.

topics.title: Reliability Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reliability Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Reliability Improvement

**Reliability Improvement** is the systematic, quantitative process of enhancing a system's probability of performing its intended function without failure, over a specified period and under stated operating conditions. It is achieved by reducing the **hazard rate** ($\lambda$), mitigating failure modes, and adding **redundant** elements so that the Mean Time Between Failures (**MTBF**) and the system Reliability function $R(t)$ both increase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PERUT636, Module 4):**
> Reliability improvement refers to engineering, design, and operational interventions that increase the **survival probability** of a system. The gain is quantified using the dimensionless **Reliability Improvement Factor (RIF)**.

$$RIF = \frac{\lambda_{old}}{\lambda_{new}} = \frac{MTBF_{new}}{MTBF_{old}} \quad \text{where} \quad RIF > 1$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Spare Tyre on a Highway Drive"

Imagine you are driving from Kottayam to Kasaragod on a single tyre. The probability of reaching the destination is low. Now picture a car with **two tyres mounted simultaneously** (both touching the road) — even if one blows out, the car continues. This is **Active Redundancy**.

Now imagine a car with **one tyre in use and a spare tyre locked in the boot**. The spare is *not* stressed until needed; it is activated only when the active tyre fails. This is **Standby Redundancy**. Because the spare suffers no wear, it offers a *higher* reliability gain per unit cost than the active case.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Reliability improvement is essentially a *trade-off game* between **Cost**, **Weight**, **Volume**, **Power Consumption**, and **System Reliability**. The engineer's job is to find the *optimum redundancy level* — the point beyond which adding more components provides diminishing returns.

### 1.3 Classification of Reliability Improvement Methods

| # | Method Category | Engineering Lever |
|---|-----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Inherent Improvement** | Better components, derating, design simplification |
| 2 | **Redundancy** | Active / Standby / Component / System redundancy |
| 3 | **Environmental Control** | Temperature, humidity, vibration, EMI shielding |
| 4 | **Maintenance Strategy** | Preventive, predictive, condition-based maintenance |
| 5 | **Manufacturing & Screening** | Burn-in testing, ESS (Environmental Stress Screening) |

### 1.4 Quantitative Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Remarks |
|--------|--------|------|---------|
| Failure Rate | $\lambda$ | failures/hour | Assumed **constant** in this module |
| Mean Time To Failure | MTTF | hours | For non-repairable systems |
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | hours | For repairable systems |
| Reliability Improvement Factor | RIF | dimensionless | Ratio of new to old performance |
| Availability | $A(t)$ | dimensionless | $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners consistently award full marks only when students explicitly state the **assumptions** (constant $\lambda$, statistically independent components, perfect switching for standby, etc.) before substituting into formulas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Reliability Improvement

#### Pillar 1 — Component Selection & Derating
Selecting components whose rated specifications (voltage, current, temperature) significantly exceed the operating stress is called **Derating**. A widely accepted industry rule is the **Arrhenius-type relation** for electronic components:

$$AF = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{k}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

where $AF$ is the *Acceleration Factor*, $E_a$ the activation energy (eV), $k$ Boltzmann's constant ($8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K), and $T_2 < T_1$ are absolute temperatures (K).

#### Pillar 2 — Design Simplification
The single greatest enemy of reliability is *unnecessary complexity*. Every additional part introduces a multiplicative reliability penalty in series systems:

$$R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

#### Pillar 3 — Active (Parallel) Redundancy
All $n$ identical components operate simultaneously. The system survives if **at least one** unit works.

$$R_{parallel}(t) = 1 - (1 - R(t))^n$$

For exponential components:

$$R_{parallel}(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$

#### Pillar 4 — Standby (Cold Spare) Redundancy
Only one unit operates; spares are switched in upon failure of the active unit. With perfect switching and $k$ spares, the system reliability becomes the probability that **fewer than $(k+1)$** failures occur in time $t$ (Poisson statistics):

$$R_{standby}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \sum_{i=0}^{k} \frac{(\lambda t)^i}{i!}$$

> [!NOTE]
> With one active unit and one cold spare ($k=1$):
> $$R_{standby}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$$
> This always exceeds $2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ (the active-parallel equivalent).

#### Pillar 5 — Series-Parallel vs Parallel-Series Configurations
For four identical components of reliability $R$:

$$R_{series\text{-}parallel} = 1 - (1 - R^2)^2$$

$$R_{parallel\text{-}series} = 1 - (1 - R)^4$$

These are **never equal** except at $R = 0$ or $R = 1$. The series-parallel arrangement is *more reliable* when $R < 1$.

#### Pillar 6 — Optimum Redundancy & Cost Trade-off
The total cost of a redundant system is linear in the number of units:

$$C_{total} = C_{fixed} + n \cdot C_{component}$$

The optimum $n$ satisfies:

$$\Delta R(n) = \frac{R(n) - R(n-1)}{C(n) - C(n-1)} = \text{maximum economically feasible ratio}$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In all KTU 2024 answer sheets, always use $\vert$ for absolute values inside $\LaTeX$ formulas when copying into tables to avoid breaking the table syntax.

| S.No. | Configuration | Reliability Formula $R_s(t)$ | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single component (exponential) | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | Basic benchmark |
| 2 | Series ($n$ independent) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = \exp\!\left(-\sum \lambda_i t\right)$ | All must work |
| 3 | Active parallel ($n$ identical) | $1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$ | Any-one-works |
| 4 | Standby (1 active + $k$ spares) | $e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{i=0}^{k}(\lambda t)^i / i!$ | Cold-spare systems |
| 5 | Series-Parallel | $1 - (1 - R_1 R_2 \cdots)^m$ | Dual-channel |
| 6 | Parallel-Series | $1 - (1 - R_1)^m(1 - R_2)^n \cdots$ | Modular redundant |
| 7 | RIF (Improvement Factor) | $\lambda_{old}/\lambda_{new}$ | Quantify gain |
| 8 | Cost Function | $C_0 + nC_c$ | Economic trade-off |
| 9 | Reliability Allocation (AGREE) | $R_i = R_s^{w_i / \sum w_i}$ | Design apportionment |
| 10 | Hazard Multiplication | $\lambda_{eq} = \sum \lambda_i$ | Series constant rate |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (ISRO, NASA):** Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) with voting logic uses series-parallel redundancy.
- **Automotive ECU:** Dual-core lockstep processors use active redundancy for ISO 26262 ASIL-D compliance.
- **Telecom (BSNL/BSNL redundancy):** Hot-standby line cards in routers use $1 + 1$ standby redundancy.
- **Nuclear Power Plants (Kudankulam):** Four-train safety systems use parallel-series redundancy for fail-safe operation.
- **Defence (DRDO):** Standby redundancy in missile guidance systems — primary IMU plus cold-spare IMU activated on fault.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability of n Active Parallel Components (Identical, Exponential)

**Statement:** Derive the reliability of a system where $n$ statistically independent, identical components with constant failure rate $\lambda$ are connected in active parallel.

**Assumption List (mandatory in KTU answers):**
1. Components are statistically independent.
2. Failure rate $\lambda$ is **constant** (exponential distribution).
3. All $n$ components operate simultaneously from $t = 0$.

**Logical Flow:**

- **Step 1 — Probability of failure of one component in time $t$:**
  $$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

- **Step 2 — Probability that all $n$ components fail simultaneously (system failure):**
  By independence,
  $$F_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} F_i(t) = [F(t)]^n = (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$

- **Step 3 — System reliability is the complement of system failure:**
  $$R_s(t) = 1 - F_s(t)$$
  
  Substituting Step 2:
  $$R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$

- **Step 4 — Binomial Expansion for explicit terms:**
  Using $(1 - x)^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n}\binom{n}{k}(-x)^k$ with $x = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s(t) &= 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{n}\binom{n}{k}(-1)^k e^{-k\lambda t} \\
&= -\sum_{k=1}^{n}\binom{n}{k}(-1)^k e^{-k\lambda t} \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{n}\binom{n}{k}(-1)^{k+1} e^{-k\lambda t}
\end{aligned}$$

- **Step 5 — Limiting Cases (Sanity Check):**
  - $n=1$: $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}$ ✓ (degenerates to single component)
  - $n\to\infty$: $R_s \to 1$ ✓ (infinite redundancy gives perfect reliability)

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reliability of Standby Redundancy (1 Active + $k$ Spares, Exponential)

**Statement:** Derive $R_s(t)$ for a system with 1 active component and $k$ identical cold-standby spares activated sequentially upon failure (perfect switching, $\lambda$ constant).

**Assumption List:**
1. Components are identical with failure rate $\lambda$.
2. Spares are inert until switched in (no wear).
3. Switch is **perfect** (zero failure probability, zero switching time).
4. Failures occur as a **Poisson process** with rate $\lambda$.

**Logical Flow:**

- **Step 1 — Failure as a Poisson process:**
  The number of failures in $(0, t]$ is a Poisson random variable $N(t)$ with parameter $\mu = \lambda t$.
  $$P[N(t) = k] = \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

- **Step 2 — System survives if total failures $\leq k$:**
  The system fails only after the **$(k+1)$-th** failure (when all $k+1$ units have failed).
  $$R_s(t) = P[N(t) \leq k] = \sum_{i=0}^{k} \frac{(\lambda t)^i e^{-\lambda t}}{i!}$$

- **Step 3 — Factoring out the exponential:**
  $$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{i=0}^{k} \frac{(\lambda t)^i}{i!}$$

- **Step 4 — Verification for $k = 0$ (no spare):**
  $$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot 1 = e^{-\lambda t} \checkmark$$

- **Step 5 — Verification for $k = 1$ (one cold spare):**
  $$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$$

- **Step 6 — Comparison with active parallel ($n=2$):**
  $$R_{active}(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$$
  $$R_{standby}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda t e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$$
  
  Numerical comparison at $\lambda t = 0.5$:
  - Active: $2(0.6065) - 0.3679 = 0.8451$
  - Standby: $0.6065 \times 1.5 = 0.9098$ ✓ (standby is superior)

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reliability Improvement Factor (RIF)

**Statement:** Show that $RIF = \lambda_{old}/\lambda_{new}$ in terms of reliabilities at a fixed mission time $t$.

**Logical Flow:**

- **Step 1 — Define new and old reliabilities:**
  $$R_{new}(t) = e^{-\lambda_{new} t}, \qquad R_{old}(t) = e^{-\lambda_{old} t}$$

- **Step 2 — Solve for failure rates:**
  $$\lambda_{new} = -\frac{\ln R_{new}}{t}, \qquad \lambda_{old} = -\frac{\ln R_{old}}{t}$$

- **Step 3 — Form the ratio:**
$$\begin{aligned}
RIF &= \frac{\lambda_{old}}{\lambda_{new}} \\
&= \frac{-\ln R_{old}/t}{-\ln R_{new}/t} \\
&= \frac{\ln R_{old}}{\ln R_{new}}
\end{aligned}$$

- **Step 4 — Equivalent MTBF formulation:**
  Since $MTBF = 1/\lambda$:
  $$RIF = \frac{1/\lambda_{new}}{1/\lambda_{old}} = \frac{MTBF_{new}}{MTBF_{old}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Optimum Redundancy from Cost Constraint

**Statement:** For a redundant system of $n$ identical components each costing $C_c$, find the smallest integer $n$ such that $R_s(n) \geq R_{required}$, subject to total cost $C_0 + nC_c \leq C_{budget}$.

**Logical Flow:**

- **Step 1 — Write the cost constraint:**
  $$n \leq \frac{C_{budget} - C_0}{C_c} = n_{max}$$

- **Step 2 — Write the reliability requirement:**
  $$R_s(n) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n \geq R_{required}$$

- **Step 3 — Solve for $n$ (algebraic manipulation):**
$$1 - R_{required} \geq (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$
$$n \geq \frac{\ln(1 - R_{required})}{\ln(1 - e^{-\lambda t})}$$

- **Step 4 — Choose minimum integer $n$:**
  $$n_{opt} = \min\left\{\left\lceil\frac{\ln(1 - R_{required})}{\ln(1 - e^{-\lambda t})}\right\rceil, \; \left\lfloor\frac{C_{budget} - C_0}{C_c}\right\rfloor\right\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **closed-form expression** is heavily favoured by KTU examiners because it permits direct numerical substitution.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Redundancy

| Decision Criterion | Active Parallel | Standby (Cold) | Standby (Hot) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switching device reliability | Not required | Must be very high | Must be very high |
| Component wear during standby | Yes (continuous) | No | Yes (continuous) |
| Power consumption | $n \times$ single | $\approx 1 \times$ single | $n \times$ single |
| Reliability gain (per added unit) | Moderate | **Highest** | Moderate |
| Detection of failed unit | Hard (must be online) | **Easy** | Hard |
| Best for | Short missions, repairable systems | Long missions, unrepairable | Critical safety loops |

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term **Reliability Improvement Factor (RIF)**. A component originally had an MTBF of 500 hours. After derating, the new MTBF is 2000 hours. Calculate the RIF and the percentage improvement in reliability at mission time $t = 100$ hours, assuming constant failure rate.*

**Model Answer:**

> The **Reliability Improvement Factor (RIF)** is the dimensionless ratio of the improved performance metric to the original performance metric. For failure rate it is inverted; for MTBF it is direct.
> $$RIF = \frac{MTBF_{new}}{MTBF_{old}} = \frac{2000}{500} = 4$$
> The improvement factor is **4** — meaning the new system is **four times** more reliable in MTBF terms.
>
> Computing reliabilities at $t = 100$ hrs:
> $$R_{old} = e^{-100/500} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$
> $$R_{new} = e^{-100/2000} = e^{-0.05} = 0.9512$$
>
> Percentage improvement:
> $$\%\text{Improvement} = \frac{R_{new} - R_{old}}{R_{old}} \times 100 = \frac{0.9512 - 0.8187}{0.8187} \times 100 \approx 16.18\%$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between **Active Redundancy** and **Standby Redundancy**. State two practical engineering systems where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Active Redundancy | Standby Redundancy |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | All units run simultaneously | One runs, others wait |
| Switching | Not required | Essential (perfect switch) |
| Power | $n \times$ single unit | $\approx 1 \times$ single unit |
| Wear | Continuous on all | Only on active until failure |
| Reliability gain | Moderate | **Higher per unit cost** |

**Practical applications:**

- **Active redundancy:** Aircraft twin-engine systems, dual-processor ECUs, dual-channel railway signalling relays.
- **Standby redundancy:** Telecom hot-standby line cards (with sensing), satellite transponder backup, hospital UPS-battery systems.
